author: @karlswanson95
text:
Nice to see.  

Different idea related to batteries.  
If you have two granular (sand like) materials with different electronegativity (aluminum vs lead) and you had them mixed in some electrolyte solution (just wet).  Imagine there are collector plates on the top and bottom of the mix.  Is there any chance you could generate electricity as the particles moved up or down (because of liquefaction) based on their density and difference in electronegativity?  Just a thought.  If it worked it would only produce when in a state of liquefaction.  To recharge just flip around.

The fan has 3 speeds so you could test the original and tubercle versions at different speeds. I would also try to make some sound measurements. author: @hunnybunnysheavymetalmusic6542
text:
Heating-and-Cooling furnaces/heat pumps have fans in them, and they're generally used during both winter and summer, so there's no reason to say its not of any value.

Even as I type this, I have a fan blowing on me, just because I like the feel of the air gently blowing across my skin like a breeze.

If a fan has a speed setting low enough, it can be fairly pleasant for some people to have a fan on while they sleep.

My mother almost can't sleep at all without a fan on her face, and I've met others like that.

author: @HHOGAS
text: The reason why your fan worked better is you had more pitch to the blade that grab more air. Look at the comparison on the pitch on the white fan blade versus the one you made and you can see the pitch is different determines the air it grabs. Put it in the regular fan motor and if the pitch is too great and motors not strong enough it will crate resistance on the motor and put a drag on the motor because the motor will not be strong enough to supersede the increase pitch on the one you built and it might be the same amount of air due to air drag on the pitch.

author: @brntuk
text: I'm guessing this all comes from biomimicry. (Read The Sharks Paintbrush by Jay Harman.) author: @marcfruchtman9473
text:
I wouldn't count this as a fair test and demo for the tubercle blade... for one thing, you have many many more blades on the tubercle fan design. So you can't really say it was the tubercles that did the improvement. A better test would simply be printing the same blade with and without the tubercles.
   Re: measuring the flow of air: What I did for my own purposes was to create a tapered funnel with guide baffles (to prevent vortices) that had the ~blade diameter as the input and tapered down to the shape and diameter of the Anemometer for the output. That way I get more consistent flow rates. You might have to have several adapters for different fan sizes. If you just put the wind tester in different places you tend to get different readings. The readings using the funnel method seem much more consistent tho they will change with barometric pressure from day to day. You can easily 3D Print such a system.

   Note: wind vortices inside the funnel will tend to spin the meter more or less...which can skew the results, which is why I add the baffles into the design. For more stringent testing, avoid using a drill in forward for 1 blade and reverse for the other, because the drill speed won't be consistent unless you control for that.

author: @gerryjamesedwards1227
text: It seems to have excellent slow-speed efficiency, which would make it far quieter than the other fan, for the same performance, as it can do it at a slower speed. That's assuming they're both as noisy as each other, which doesn't seem to be true, although most of what I could hear was drill motor to be honest. Even if you can't sell these, the fact that you can DIY a better fan than you can buy is another excellent reason to get into 3D printing.

author: @johndough8115
text:
Your "TEST" is complete and utter SHITE.   You have the technical ability,  and know-how,  to make a FAR better test situation.   Its as easy as printing the exact same blades with a standard shape.. placing them centered into a tube.. and reading the wind speed at the other end of the tube.   This would not only assure that the data is realistically ACCURATE..  but also, by what Percentages.

 For example... does the wind speed only go up by a mere 2%... but the additional Weight of the blades, balance out the performance in Energy consumption?

 You are comparing two VERY different types of fans here... and not in an Optimal way.   The larger 3 bladed fan,  needs to be spun at much higher speeds, to maintain good airflow.    The fan with multiple small blades, with less space between each blade... can be rotated at much less speed... and produce much more airflow AT those low speeds.   BUT...  if you tried to spin that same fan at higher speeds... you might find that the LARGER 3 BLADED FAN... actually FAR out-performs it,  in the AMOUNT of Cubic feet per minute, of airflow.

 The thing is... at certain speeds,  certain fans can and do create Turbulence, which can actually cause wind to be pushed out of both sides of the fan... or at very least... cause less than Optimal airflow results.

 In my case... I bought some surplus fans that came out of Ancient HP printers.  They were AC fans.  About 6 inch x 6 inch.  There were three 1.5 ? inch wide blades.  And the blades were like 1.5" deep.   Much deeper / thicker  than typical PC fans.    These things were LOUD as hell... and they spun fast enough to cut carrots into mush, without slowing down.   I had placed them in from of a modified PC case.  Two of them stacked in front of the case... made the entire case like a Hurricane... and actually a bit too strong.

 I had smaller and larger sized PC fans... that had far more blades... but they couldnt move a FRACTION of the amount of Air that those 3 bladed fans moved,  at that rotational speed (which I believe was MUCH faster than any of my PC fans).

 Application also matters.   A Blower type of fan,  has many small blades... but they pressurize and move air a lot differently... due to the Housing difference.

 So... Fans appear to designed to operate at certain rotation speed range... and are specifically designed with a certain number of blades, with the size.. shape.. angles... and mass of the blades... all takes into carful consideration, for its intended Optimal Operational form.   Its not as simple as just printing something up... and then dropping it on any old motor,  in any type of housing,  at any speed you desire.
